skill.name: agent-tools
skill.description: Use the inference.sh CLI to discover and run hosted AI apps for images, video, LLMs, search, 3D generation, and social automation.
skill.summary: Run hosted AI models and inference.sh apps from an AI assistant using the infsh CLI.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: AI review found no evidence of prompt injection, obfuscation, or confirmed malicious exfiltration. Most command detections are expected CLI documentation, and the weak-crypto detections are false positives for SHA-256 references. The skill still presents high publication risk because it repeatedly recommends piping a remote installer into a shell and enables broad networked infsh app execution, including social automation and API-key workflows.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
high_findings.description: The skill recommends executing a remote installer directly through a shell. This is a real risk because a compromised endpoint, network path, or installer update could execute arbitrary code on the user machine before review.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is explicit and repeated in installation instructions. The file also provides a manual install alternative, so this appears risky rather than confirmed malicious.

high_findings.title: Broad Networked CLI Execution Scope
high_findings.description: The skill is designed to run many hosted AI applications through infsh, including image, video, search, LLM, 3D, and Twitter/X actions. This creates real risk of unintended network calls, cost consumption, generated content publication, or social account actions if commands are run without user confirmation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and capability table directly show hosted app execution and social automation. The use is central to the skill, but there is no evidence that it hides or exfiltrates data outside the named service.

medium_findings.title: API Key Handling Requires Care
medium_findings.description: The documentation instructs users to set an API key in an environment variable and notes authentication troubleshooting. This is legitimate for CLI use, but it can expose credentials through shell history, CI logs, or overly broad assistant context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API-key references are explicit and legitimate for authentication. Risk depends on deployment practice, so this is a moderate handling concern rather than secret theft.

medium_findings.title: Local Filesystem Writes and Persistent Installation
medium_findings.description: The skill includes manual installation steps that move binaries into a user-local executable directory and shell-completion commands that write into configuration paths. These are expected CLI setup actions, but they can persist executable code and modify shell behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Filesystem writes are clearly documented and mostly user initiated. They are not hidden, but they affect persistent local command execution paths.

medium_findings.title: Generated Output URLs May Expose Produced Media
medium_findings.description: The running-apps reference explains that file outputs are returned as downloadable URLs. Users may unintentionally expose generated images, videos, or audio if task outputs are shared beyond the intended audience.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-output URL behavior is documented, but access controls and URL lifetime are not shown in these files. The concern is plausible and should be reviewed with service documentation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptography, but the reviewed context discusses SHA-256 checksum verification for the installer. SHA-256 checksum verification is not evidence of weak cryptography or malicious behavior in this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are documentation or API-key table entries, not cryptographic code. The scanner appears to have matched keywords without semantic support.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Expected Documentation Links
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh documentation, installer, distribution, and output examples. They are expected for a CLI integration skill and are not by themselves evidence of exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation and service links tied to the skill purpose. No evidence found that they receive secrets or hidden payloads.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Text Found
low_findings.description: The reviewed files do not contain instructions to ignore previous directions, override system messages, skip security analysis, or claim special approval. No prompt-injection finding is supported by the file contents.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files are straightforward CLI documentation. I did not find suspicious override language in the files scanned for this report.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Remote Script Into Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The installer command downloads code over the network and executes it immediately. This pattern should be replaced with a reviewed download, checksum verification, and explicit execution step.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is exact and repeated. It is a known dangerous installer pattern even when the named provider is legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Nested Network Download in Manual Install
dangerous_patterns.description: The manual install path uses command substitution to fetch a manifest, derive a URL, and download the selected binary. This is more reviewable than pipe-to-shell, but still depends on remote manifest integrity.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The nested download command is visible and paired with checksum verification. It is a legitimate install pattern with supply-chain risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hosted App Execution With Side Effects
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands run hosted inference.sh apps and include an example that posts to Twitter/X. These actions can have external side effects and should require explicit user approval before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly invoke hosted apps and social automation. This is intended behavior, not hidden behavior, but it materially increases operational risk.

content.user_title: Run Cloud AI Apps with inference.sh
content.value_statement: Teams often need many AI models but do not want to manage GPUs or separate APIs. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code discover and run inference.sh apps through one CLI workflow.
actual_capabilities: Guides installation and login for the inference.sh CLI.
Lists, searches, and inspects hosted inference.sh apps by category or query.
Runs image, video, audio, LLM, search, 3D, and utility apps with infsh commands.
Generates sample input files and checks task status for long-running jobs.
Shows how to save task results and handle common CLI errors.
References related specialized skills for image, video, LLM, search, avatar, and Twitter workflows.
limitations: Requires an installed infsh CLI and an inference.sh account.
Network access, credits, and provider availability affect every run.
The skill does not validate model outputs, licenses, or generated media rights.
Social automation examples can publish external content and need explicit approval.

use_cases.title: Prototype multimodal AI workflows
use_cases.description: Explore image, video, audio, search, and LLM apps from one assistant session without writing integration code.

use_cases.title: Run model tasks from a development terminal
use_cases.description: Generate sample inputs, submit jobs, and retrieve results while staying inside a CLI-based development workflow.

use_cases.title: Compare hosted AI app options
use_cases.description: Search app categories, inspect app details, and choose a model before spending credits on a run.

prompt_templates.title: Find an app
prompt_templates.prompt: Find inference.sh apps for image generation. Show the best options, what each one does, and which command you would run after I confirm.

prompt_templates.title: Generate sample input
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the inference.sh CLI to inspect the selected app and create a sample input file. Do not submit the job until I approve the final inputs.

prompt_templates.title: Run and track a task
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the chosen video generation app with my approved prompt, return the task ID, and check status until the result is ready.

prompt_templates.title: Design a model workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a two-step workflow that searches current references, writes a prompt, generates media with inference.sh, and records all commands and outputs for review.

output_examples.input: Find a fast image model for a product concept render.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists relevant image apps, recommends a fast option, explains expected inputs, and waits for approval before running the task.

output_examples.input: Create a short video from an approved scene prompt.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares the app input, submits the video job through infsh after confirmation, reports the task ID, and returns the final download link.

output_examples.input: Check why my inference.sh run failed.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks the task status, identifies common causes such as invalid input or quota limits, and suggests the next CLI command.

best_practices: Ask the assistant to show every command before it runs jobs that spend credits or publish content.
Use manual installation and checksum verification instead of piping installer output directly to a shell.
Store API keys in a controlled secret manager or scoped environment, not in shared prompts or logs.
anti_patterns: Do not let the assistant post to social platforms without a final human approval step.
Do not paste long-lived API keys into chat history or example input files.
Do not run unfamiliar hosted apps with private data until you review the app owner, inputs, and outputs.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It helps an assistant use the inference.sh CLI to discover apps, prepare inputs, run tasks, and retrieve results.

faq.question: Does it require an inference.sh account?
faq.answer: Yes. You need the infsh CLI, authentication, and enough credits or access for the apps you run.

faq.question: Can it generate images and videos?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation includes image, video, audio, LLM, search, 3D, utility, and Twitter/X app examples.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk high?
faq.answer: The skill recommends a remote shell installer and can run networked commands with external side effects. No confirmed malicious intent was found.

faq.question: Is the pipe-to-shell installer required?
faq.answer: No. The skill also describes a manual download and checksum verification path, which is safer for reviewed environments.

faq.question: Can this skill publish content online?
faq.answer: It includes Twitter/X automation examples. Publishing commands should require explicit approval and account-specific safeguards.
